questions.question: Why should storage design start with access patterns instead of table names?
options: Because the main queries determine which keys, indexes, and denormalizations are worth paying for.
Because interviewers usually refuse to discuss databases until the end.
Because access patterns eliminate the need for capacity estimation.
Because schema choices are mostly cosmetic and easy to reverse later.
questions.explanation: The cost profile of a data model is driven by the reads and writes it must serve well.

questions.question: Which query should most strongly influence the primary serving model?
options: The rare export path requested once per quarter.
The query with the highest combined business criticality and traffic.
The most complex query the analytics team can imagine.
Any query that sounds hard enough to impress the interviewer.
questions.explanation: Primary storage should fit the dominant and most important request path, not the fanciest one.

questions.question: What is the main reason to move exports and bulk scans to asynchronous jobs?
options: It protects the serving path from long scans and unpredictable contention.
It guarantees all exports will be strongly consistent with every replica.
It removes the need for authentication and authorization checks.
It allows the primary database to skip backups.
questions.explanation: Async paths isolate heavy work from latency-sensitive online traffic.

questions.question: Which metric most directly tests whether the team ranked access patterns correctly?
options: The number of meetings held by the schema review group.
Latency and volume grouped by named access pattern.
Only total CPU usage on the storage fleet.
The average number of columns per table.
questions.explanation: Per-pattern observability shows whether the designed hot path is actually the real hot path.

questions.question: What is the hidden trade-off of adding a secondary index for every plausible filter?
options: Reads become impossible on replicas.
Writes become more expensive and the model becomes harder to operate safely.
Primary keys are no longer required.
Client retries stop working.
questions.explanation: Every extra index consumes write budget, storage, and operational attention.

questions.question: When should a rare but valuable query influence the primary schema anyway?
options: When it is costly to explain why it is rare.
When it is actually business-critical enough that degrading it would break the product.
When it requires the most tables in the schema diagram.
When it is easier than building a derived system.
questions.explanation: Criticality can outweigh low frequency if the business truly depends on the path.

questions.question: What is the best senior-level response when an interviewer asks for keyword search on top of OLTP data?
options: Put full-text search directly into the primary serving table by default.
Reject search entirely because it is outside the prompt.
Consider a derived search system if keyword search is important enough to justify separate indexing and lag.
Store every field twice in the primary store and search by substring.
questions.explanation: A derived system is often the cleanest way to support search without distorting the OLTP model.

questions.question: What outcome most strongly suggests the team optimized for the wrong access pattern?
options: The rare admin query is elegant, but the main user read path now has unstable tail latency.
The system uses a compound primary key.
The architecture includes one asynchronous job.
The schema has fewer tables than expected.
questions.explanation: When the core path suffers to make rare paths nicer, the prioritization is wrong.
